translation.content: If the electronic music industry is caught in the crosshairs of a battle over what makes for true techno, then D.Dan is one of the underground's great modern emissaries. A figurehead from the new guard of DJs to arise in the '20s, the Berlin-based artist and Mala Junta resident is an ambassador for a sound that is strongly anchored in the classic roots of techno. His productions, like his mixes, are revved-up takes on the hypnotic wormholes that defined dance floors last decade, but with a fresh, high-BPM twist. Originally from Seattle, the birthplace of grunge and second-wave coffee culture, D.Dan became enamoured with the spectral stylings of psych rock and shoegaze in adolescence. It's not difficult to see how the cosmic tapestry of bands like Cocteau Twins became a blueprint for the entrancing music he's gone on to make as an adult. His releases on Mutant Future and summerpup are latticeworks of loops and layered percussion, custom-tooled for lost hours on the dance floor and drawn-out mixes behind the decks. When asked about the origins of the circular style that's now become his calling card, he says that it's been the propitious results of letting go and developing a super-streamlined configuration of hardware. Synth hooks hatched from a Cirklon sequencer at the heart of his setup form the backbone of his tracks, unfurling in endless, undulating waves that have the phenomenal quality of feeling thematically tight without sounding the same. This approach directly extends into his DJ practice, where he pairs song selections from contemporaries that mirror his own reduced, controlled approach to techno. RA.994 is a Grade A display of contemporary four-to-the-floor from flagbearers like Roll Dann and Marcal. And like D.Dan's standalone records, his RA Podcast finds room for sweetness—the intermittent peal of an open clap, the steady ripening of a chord—while ultimately emphasising the beauty of function and form over flair. - Chloe Lula What have you been up to recently? Over the last six months I've been traveling and touring a lot, but finding a nice balance in it all between gigging and time for my personal life. Since December I've been to North America for my end-of-year tour and time with family over the holidays, then to Asia to play some really nice gigs in Chengdu for the annual Chinese New Year celebrations at .TAG, where I hold a residency, as well as playing in Thailand and Tokyo. After that, I went to Mexico solely to travel, then returned back to Europe for a month of gigs before going back to Japan for further travels. Then I went onwards to North America again in May for another tour to play club and DIY gigs as well as festivals like WIRE in New York and Movement in Detroit. I also got some studio time in Oakland to work on a project my best friend and I have started together. Needless to say it's been a pretty wild ride the last six months but it's been extremely fulfilling and inspiring both musically and personally. I feel very grateful for the opportunities and moments I've had with my gigs, as well as being able to spend a lot of quality time and travels with my loved ones. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? The mix was recorded in my studio in Berlin on June 12th, 2025. It's a studio variation of the set I played at my debut performance at Movement Festival in Detroit this year at the Underground Stage. Due to several circumstances, there was a lot I had processed in my inner world in the week preceding the gig. During the set, I was able to find strong catharsis and connection with the energy in the room and the crowd. I'm still carrying that energy, inspiration and my personal experience in Detroit with me to this day. The idea behind this mix was to record a version of it within a studio context to encapsulate this moment in time. Also so that people who connected with it in Detroit can re-live it in some kind of way. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? I would have to say that it was my general experience of being a part of the local scene and community in Seattle from 2012 to 2016. But there was one specific event where I saw Voices From The Lake (Donato Dozzy and Neel) play a live set, with local DJ Eddie Lee opening, in a DIY warehouse venue called Big Building that sat in an underpass in Seattle in (I believe) 2013. It was put on by a promoter called High & Tight. The only way to get tickets was through private mailing list RSVP and people had to vouch for you to get an invite. It was the first proper warehouse techno party I'd ever been to, a friend had an extra ticket and had last-minute invited me to go the night of. I remember driving down to the industrial district by the harbour in Seattle and having to stop at the train tracks and wait for a very long freight train to pass before finally being able to find the venue tucked away under the highway bridge. It was the first time I'd experienced such hypnosis from techno and collective dance in a very dark venue. I also vividly remember the way Eddie Lee opened that night and was patiently building up layers, tension and anticipation as the party really got started, creating these moments when people came together and locked into the dance floor and all started screaming. Something clicked for me that night and I just knew I had to dig deeper into what this music is all about. That was what also led me into meeting Eddie Lee, as well as the collective he was running called secondnature and eventually joining their collective as my first residency as a techno DJ. You just put out a release of non-dance floor music. What can you express in non-dance floor music that you can't in techno? I would say the first track on the single, "Embers," is still dance-floor music, albeit in a pretty unconventional way. The solo guitar track, "As The Desert Exhales," is indeed pretty much as far away from club music as a song could be. With non-dance floor oriented music, when I'm making an instrumental song with guitar for example, there isn't really any function I'm going for beyond purely conveying an emotion or a mood. In that process, I'm not really concerned about the song fitting any kind of format or context aside from what I feel in that moment. The time and space I use for making non-dance floor music is pretty intimate in that way, because I'm not making it with any intention to play live or necessarily to even release it. A song is more encapsulated within itself. When I'm playing a physical instrument, it's more natural to spontaneously play tempo changes, accents or nuanced phrases without having to programme them—the instrument is right there at the fingertips. It's more of an organic process. But when I'm producing techno, I'm working with machines and more closely with the computer. The process is intrinsically machine-driven, more programmatic and more abstract. There's an element of functionality for making tracks that are ultimately for a club context and to be played by DJs, which informs the production process in several ways: arrangement, engineering, tempo, sound-system physics, track length and so on. There's more of a balance to strike between all of that and the human emotion, mood and energy I'm trying to convey in a dance floor track. Sometimes, the point of an exercise is to channel these machines to convey something completely ice-cold and robotic, for example like in my track "Pins and Needles"—a kind of expression that is much harder to be done naturally with an instrument like a guitar. They are two very different processes, but I appreciate and find different kinds of creativity from both, with their own strengths and limitations. Bouncing between these two processes keeps things refreshing for me and these different workflows do cross over and influence each other in an interesting way. Your calling card is loopy, high-BPM techno. When and how did you latch onto that sound? I would say it's a combination of several factors that came together over many years. In the earlier stages of when I was digging into techno, I was primarily into a slower, deeper style; stuff that was put out on labels like Prologue, Northern Electronics and Edit Select in the early to mid-2010s. When I first started visiting Berlin from 2013 onwards, I'd go to record shops every chance I could get—especially Record Loft. It was there that I started to dig and find more records of faster, raw and punchy old school sounds from the '90s and '00s. I liked how raw, percussive and energetic these old-school records were: stuff that was released on labels like Purpose Maker, Downwards and Blueprint for example. I'd say my sound today is very much a coming together of all of these influences. What kinds of music are you most drawn to right now as a DJ and a listener? The kinds of sounds I'm drawn to the most right now as a DJ are fairly well represented in the RA Podcast. Textural, loopy, hypnotic, bassline-driven techno. I dig for all kinds of music across genres, tempos and grooves, and right now I'm particularly connecting with music that has those qualities. As a listener, I've been listening primarily to ambient, post-rock and shoegaze. Some examples of bands who recently released albums I really love are So Totally, Been Stellar and Pure Hex. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Cooking my favourite Korean dishes at home in Berlin. Studio time. And all of my upcoming gigs. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Representation and visibility of Asian artists.
translation.blurb: Waves of pulsing, layered techno from the revered Mala Junta resident.
